item.title: Breaking through structures of power
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

I grew up in a typical patriarchal family in which the men ‘provided’, they made the important decisions, and they got the leg piece in the chicken curry. However, what always took me by surprise, even as a little girl, was my grandmother who orchestrated this patriarchal system. She laboured all day in the kitchen, had no wishes or dreams beyond those four walls, and was content as long as she ensured my mother’s life was always a little unhappy.

I remember her grinding her teeth and muttering under her breath with great disappointment (possibly in herself) every time I served myself the chicken leg before she could serve it to my brother. As a child, I only did this to irk her and be amused by why this would be such a bother, but I get it today.

Power structures work exactly like this. Those who have been victims of oppression cannot perceive the structures that their oppressors have created. They do not perceive themselves as oppressed and their existential experience adopts an attitude of “adhesion” to their oppressor (Friere, 1970). My poor grandmother was a victim of this system.

Revolutionary Brazilian educational theorist, Paulo Freire, in his book Pedagogy of the Oppressed wrote extensively about this. Freire viewed society as a dynamic, ever-evolving system through which power is woven. That power, sometimes intentionally, sometimes unintentionally, oppresses, creating social structures, institutions, ideas, and myths that sustain the wealth, way of life, and power of oppressors. All this comes at the expense of the oppressed. In the context of education, it comes at the expense of knowledge and the process of inquiry.


Power structures in education

Teachers have innate power in the classroom. The fact that they are an adult “in-charge” of a group of young people sets a default power dynamic in favour of the teacher, even if the teacher may not want it! In a teacher-centric system, the teacher ‘teaches’ and the student is ‘taught’, the teacher ‘gives’ while the students ‘take’. There is an assumption that the teacher knows better, so they choose the content to be taught, and they set all the standards, which the student must simply measure up to.

While it may seem that the teacher holds high degrees of power (which, no doubt, they do) we cannot ignore the reality that as part of this system the teacher, too, feels as trapped and stifled as the student because of the rigidity in the structure that they have to work within. In large private  schools, teachers are literally handed out lesson plans that they must thoughtlessly execute, created by individuals sitting in corporate offices, who have little or no experience teaching in a classroom. While in the public sector, politicians and bureaucrats decide what should be taught and banned in the class.

Power structures are complex, layered, and political. While the teacher is an oppressor in one situation, they are victims of oppression in the larger system. In either situation, their autonomy is stripped away to a point that they can’t even see the oppression they face or impose. Making teachers aware of this innate power dynamic in the educational system is imperative if we would like to shift from status quo towards social change.

Theatre practitioner, Augusto Boal, influenced by Freire’s work, developed interactive and reflective theatre techniques in the 1970s, which he called the Theatre of the Oppressed (TO), to encourage audiences to be active participants in order to explore, analyze, and transform the oppression they had accepted as a way of life.

As a trained TO facilitator and trainer, I use Boal’s techniques in my workshops with teachers to better understand power. Teachers experience through the body and dynamic engagements, the imbalance of power between two bodies and the constant tension that it brews between them. It almost immediately brings perspective, and they are able to draw a parallel of how this plays out in the classroom and in the larger education system as well.

While changes in the larger system will always take more time and sustained effort, making shifts and breaking default patterns within our classrooms is not beyond our means as teachers. Reclaiming our autonomy within our own spaces is a good place to begin. Through the body and through TO exercises, teachers identify where in our classrooms we see power and the various ways of breaking or neutralizing it.

Where does power exist?
In the body

Our body has its own language and as someone who is always being watched in a classroom, acute awareness of what the body is saying is a crucial element for a teacher to be mindful about. This is also why preschool teachers are always seen sitting on their knees when they talk to children one-on-one. A stiff chin or simply arms crossed in front of the chest can send shivers down a young spine, while a body leaning against a wall casually can bring immediate ease in a room.

The body carries power, but it also has its own intelligence. By consistently engaging with the body through theatre and the arts, we can hone that intelligence and use it actively and consciously not to oppress but to enable, by knowing when to neutralize its default power.

In space

Spaces hold power too. In an auditorium, the stage draws power while the audience seating does not. However, throw in some differently coloured chairs marked with ‘reserved’ tags in the first row and you suddenly see more power on those seats than the rest of them. This is why most conventional classrooms are arranged in ways where the teacher is always at the head of the class. You will see the dynamic completely change when a teacher, even for just a moment, sits in the seat of a student.

At a school where I recently worked on power dynamics, we decided to reposition the seating arrangement of the classrooms. The teachers realized that conventional rows and columns also had a power dynamic between first benchers and last benchers, so they rearranged it such that students were seated facing each other encouraging discussions and better listening. More importantly, by doing so, the teacher had space to move around the class breaking the default position of power that they held earlier. They realized that the teacher could draw power and attention to themselves whenever they required, from anywhere in the room. This was suddenly a choice and required awareness and objective to be employed. A tangible step towards reclaiming autonomy!

In knowledge

A teacher-centric classroom, where the assumption is that the teacher knows better, projects an absolute ignorance onto the others. This is a classic characteristic of the ideology of oppression, which negates education and knowledge as a process of inquiry.

There is an evident power structure, where “the teacher confuses the authority of knowledge with his or her own professional authority, which she and he sets in opposition to the freedom of the students” (Friere, 1970). Here, there is no room to argue with the teacher, and definitely no tolerance for contradicting points of view.

The flipside to a teacher-centric classroom is a pedagogy based on an antithetical principle where students are more important than teachers a.k.a. the student-centric classroom! Here, the power lies in the hands of the students, where they are regarded as reservoirs of knowledge that needs to be tapped. Here, students decide what, how much, and at what pace they must learn, and of course, they must always be right! This dangerously tips towards becoming egocentric, where the teacher may have to yield too much of their leadership.

Shifting power to the great thing!

Educationist and philosopher Parker J Palmer, in his book The Courage to Teach beautifully presents the idea of a subject-centred classroom, where neither the teacher reigns supreme nor does the student. He says that we must put a third thing at the centre of the pedagogical circle – the subject, which he calls the great thing.

“The great thing is so alive that teacher can turn to student or student to teacher, and either can make a claim on the other in the name of that great thing. Here, teacher and students have a power beyond themselves to contend with – the power of a subject that transcends our self-absorption and refuses to be reduced to our claims about it.”

In a subject-centric classroom, the teacher’s task is to give the great thing an independent voice – a capacity to speak. When the great thing speaks for itself, the tension of power between a teacher and their students organically collapses. A subject-centric classroom does not ignore the student, but it opens up a world larger than their own experiences, egos, and personal boundaries and it is the teacher, who stimulates connections between the subject, the self, and the world, which arises from their passion for the subject.

The great thing!

In the July issue, I had touched upon the idea of power between teachers and students and how dialogue with students brings some semblance of equality in the relationship. I also extensively wrote about the importance of making connections beyond academic objectives and bringing the outside world inside the classroom. How to navigate these conversations is where the great thing lies.

A skilled preschool teacher, through a simple story about a cow causing a traffic jam, can successfully bring to life a world of facts, imagination, social norms, language, numbers, and science. A math teacher, truly passionate about their subject can help you see patterns in almost anything around you. A language teacher will not focus on the meaning of words in a poem but on that one verse, which will reveal the bare truth behind the poet’s intention, forever changing the way you read poetry.

Every discipline has a core idea, an internal logic, a patterned way of relating to the world around it, which is why disciplines are divided the way they are. Science has its own foundational system of thought, just as language does too. Teachers must invest their time in understanding the nature of their own discipline to be able to see it as this great thing that can be honoured rightfully. This is the only way in which our students can be taught “how to think like historians or biologists or literary critics rather than merely how to lip sync the conclusions others have reached” (Palmer, 1998).

Shifting focus on the subject and the nature of the discipline can develop capacities of thinking that have degenerated over decades of systemic oppression. While science should teach us scientific discoveries, it must also teach us the nature of science, because that is what will develop critical thinking. Teaching history cannot only be about the content in our textbooks constantly changing with political agendas, but must be about the nature of how history is written as well. This system of teaching and learning can liberate both the teacher and the student from the oppressive structures that limit access to knowledge, which is where power truly lies.

The writer is an arts-based therapist, educator, and children’s author. She is the former Executive Director of Akshara High School, Mumbai and has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and training teachers. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Raised in a patriarchal family, Timira observed power structures from a young age. She writes about how these dynamics manifest in classrooms, where teachers and students are caught in a web of power and control. She draws on insights from Paulo Freire and Augusto Boal to rethink the role of power in education and shows how embracing the "great thing" can liberate both teachers and students.

item.title: Decoding creativity
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

Earlier this week, in a workshop with a group of teachers, I asked them to write down values which they thought were essential for a teacher. In a matter of a few minutes a vast canvas was full of inspiring words like authentic, just, inclusive, open, punctual, clever, fun, crazy, political, and passionate. When asked to choose three values they consider core to their own teaching practice, a majority of teachers picked ‘creativity’ as one of their chosen three. Even those teachers, who at the start of the program, two years ago, had claimed that they didn’t consider themselves ‘creative’ had the word proudly written down.

This got me thinking. I firmly believe that everyone is creative, so surely, I can’t say that this was a case of transitioning from being non-creative to creative. What was this transition then? Perhaps, the only answer surfacing is that these teachers changed their perspective on what creativity meant, who is creative and what forms creativity can take. They redefined creativity, allowing them to see themselves as creative practitioners.

So how do we define creativity? The standard definition of creativity dating back to the 1930s said that creativity requires both originality and effectiveness. A definition that many scholars and philosophers adhere to even today. Originality is vital for creativity but not sufficient. Original things must be effective to be creative (Runco & Jaeger, 2012).This is interesting. A fork with a limp handle is of no use. Just like a class in which students colour ‘creative’ number worksheets with no idea that numbers carry values.

Recently, I observed a teacher in grade 3 taking an English class which was beautifully layered. It started with a simple game of blind man’s bluff that triggered rich conversations about the experience of not being able to see, bringing in personal experiences of engaging with visually impaired individuals. It then went into research tasks on famous people who were blind along with empathetic thinking tasks, considering changes they would have to make in their classroom if a blind child were to take admission in their school! All of this was an introduction to the story they were going to begin in English class on friendship, about a little girl and a visually-impaired boy.

At the end of the class, I expressed to the teacher how I was completely blown away by the seamless movement from one layer to the next and how the conversations created so much room to make varied connections, by the teacher and the students equally. The two were equally invested and curious about what was being discussed and I was amazed to see the entire class fully engaged, which is quite rare in our classrooms. She replied saying, “Earlier, I would be happy just getting them to draw a book cover for the story and be pleased with myself that I did something ‘creative’, but I now truly understand what creativity means.”

People who are creative are not afraid to question default methods, they are deeply reflective, they see the world differently, they are happy to experiment, take risks, and make mistakes. Malaguzzi stated that creativity was a way of thinking, knowing, and making choices. And when Ken Robinson talks about creativity, he talks about ‘putting your imagination to work’; he calls creativity, ‘applied imagination’.

I find the work I do fascinating. On the one hand, as part of my PhD journey, I read theories written by educators, philosophers, and scholars across several decades and various contexts. And on the other, I witness teachers playing out these very theories in their everyday lives, completely clueless about all this scholarship. Teachers sincerely put their creativity to work every single day. They use their own intuition. They are constantly thinking, knowing, and making choices in the little decisions they make and the everyday problems they solve, that perhaps goes unseen and unnoticed even by themselves.

There are several definitions of creativity, but what we can all agree on is that creativity is a process and the more we try to understand the HOW of that process, the more we can hone our creative abilities. This is what I have been trying to explore with teachers by breaking the process down to certain dispositions that we can identify and individually work on with the hope that creativity does not become a separate mental faculty but very much a part of how we think.

The disposition of playfulness builds one’s natural instincts of openness, of collaboration, of exploration, of risk taking, of problem solving, of embracing failure and uncertainty, of being aware of one’s verbal and physical communication, of persistence and determination, of fairness and justice, of being in a joyful space. All of this sharpens one’s creative thinking faculties.

How can teachers find opportunities to play regularly? The easiest way, quite obviously, is to bring play into the classroom and facilitate play with your students. But that’s not truly experiencing play. What is most essential is to build a culture of play as adults. Staff meetings can have 10-15 minutes at the start to play a quick game. Working Saturdays can have a 30-minute session where teachers can run off to the playground and decide a game, set rules, compete with each other, argue for fairness and really play! Short snatches of time to play within work hours is imperative to build this culture and disposition.

Understanding and investigating the nature of our own thinking is another habit that brings awareness to the process of thinking which can be enriched by asking the right kinds of questions, knowing in which direction to point and learning when and where to dig deeper. A math class on angles became threefold more interesting when the teacher asked the students to identify all the possible angles they could see in their classroom and then asked them to draw out all the angles that existed in their names for homework. Math is about recognizing patterns and this clearly brings the nature of the discipline to the forefront. It demonstrates that the teacher has spent time ‘thinking, knowing, and making choices’ in the questions they ask in the class – proving Malaguzzi right!

Making connections as a way of thinking creates opportunities for creative thinking to flourish. This is one of my most favourite dispositions to engage with and I’ve written about this at length in an earlier article as well titled Bringing the OUTSIDE inside the classroom. Educator and scholar, Koestler defines creativity as ‘the ability to make connections between previously unconnected ideas’. Doing this regularly as part of lessons not only enhances teachers’ creative thinking capacities but also models ways of thinking for the students they engage with. What we ‘study’ then, is not about the content dictated by the system, but an opportunity to connect with the world outside through it.

Building habits of ongoing reflection, spending time with one’s self to stay connected with our intuition, and to regularly engage with the creative arts to keep our sense of wonder and curiosity alive are ways in which we can engage all of our faculties to work together. Educator and researcher, Anna Craft, defines creativity in the most freeing way. “Creativity is a state of mind in which all of our intelligences are working together. It involves seeing, thinking and innovating. Although it is often found in the creative arts, it can be demonstrated in any subject at school or in any aspect of life.”

We have grown up around misconceptions of creativity. Those who are creative choose the arts, those who are not, must choose commerce or the sciences. It’s time to grow out of these notions. Medical practitioners are expected to be profoundly creative in order to find unique solutions to complex problems. Engineers, too, are constantly expected to re-invent. Circling back to what I said at the beginning, creativity is not seen only in “creative people”, everyone is creative. When you get home and cook up a meal with the bare minimum ingredients at the bottom of your refrigerator shelf, you are being creative.

I’d like to end this article with Anna Craft’s idea of Little ‘c’ Creativity coined to define everyday creativity:

  • as involving the active, conscious, and intentional taking of action in the world;
  • as a way of coping with everyday challenges, which may involve knowledge-based intuition just as much as step-by-step thought;
  • as involving innovation.

The focus here is on resourcefulness and agency of ordinary people, who identify problems and find innovative ways of coping with these everyday challenges. In the context of education, I find this way of defining creativity most empowering, where teachers reclaim their agency through self-determined processes, working on developing dispositions within themselves to hone their faculties towards creative thinking. They seek support in their peers and together teachers take ownership of HOW they think, know, and make decisions.

References

  • Koestler, A. (1999) The Act of Creation. Harmondsworth: Penguin.
  • Malaguzzi, L. (1996) The Hundred Languages of Children (exhibition catalogue) Reggio Emilia: Reggio Children.
  • Robinson, K (2016) Creative Schools: Penguin.
  • Craft, A. (2011) Creativity and Education Futures. Stoke-on-Trent: Trentham Books.

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
What does it truly mean to be creative? Timira writes about how redefining creativity can transform teaching.

item.title: Teacher autonomy – it’s time we claim what is rightfully ours!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

Recently, at a family wedding, I happened to catch up with a long-lost cousin who has been working on a very fascinating project. The company he works at in America (DoorDash, a food delivery app) is in the pilot phase of creating autonomous robots which are equipped to deliver food within demarcated areas. This robot can navigate traffic, both vehicular and human, follow traffic lights, know when to pull over (police car, ambulance, etc.) and when not to (fascinated passerby trying to take a selfie!). They are building the intelligence of this robot in a way that the input it keeps receiving from engaging with the world helps it build future intelligence for itself to deal with uncertainties it may face. The attempt is to make this robot autonomous with little dependency on human intervention.

This got me thinking. Isn’t this how we have always ‘learned’? Engage with the world around us. Understand it through exploration, discovery, and experimentation. And finally, use all the inputs from these engagements to keep engaging with other phenomena around you. It’s an ongoing way of being. It has always been! Every newborn child starts off their life on the planet this way. Every primary school textbook tells us how the early man did it! Yet, we fail to recognize the importance of autonomous learning systems in education. How are we to build our own learning systems and develop our innate intelligence if we are to sit inside classrooms, cut off from the world and its complexities that we are supposed to be engaging with?

Instead, we are kept busy (more than we should be) engaging with a curriculum that is often disconnected from the ongoings of the world, and dealing with superficial assessments that give you a fallacious sense of what learning looks like. This finally results in a distorted divide between “successful” learners with an inflated pride, and “failed” learners with low self-esteem; both, having built some level of trauma-bond with the idea of ‘learning’. In all of this, the teacher is left feeling exhausted, burnt-out, and completely confused about what their role has been in this process of learning for the child.

I write from the point of view of both the child learner as well as the teacher. They engage with each other as part of a system, where the primary objective is to develop learning skills and systems of intelligence within ourselves, which will help us engage with the world and its uncertain future. Pretty much what DoorDash is doing with their robots right now. However, the difference here is that we seem to have forgotten the importance of autonomy in learning. Education systems the world over have stripped the classroom of all autonomy. Both teacher and learner autonomy.

Small attempts and big successes

However, I am reminded of a bunch of extremely enthusiastic, excited, and bright-eyed government schoolteachers I met in Delhi at a conference I was attending. I have had my years of working in government schools and I can say with confidence that this bunch was an anomaly. They were strategizing on how to ensure teachers, principals, and decision-makers from outside of Delhi got a copy of the Happiness Curriculum they were distributing. I was privileged to receive a copy and have the opportunity to talk with them at length. They explained in detail what the curriculum was about, what the impact has been in class, anecdotes from students, parents, and other teachers. I loved the enthusiasm, and I was curious to know where this intense sense of ownership came from.

Slowly, I understood. The thinkers behind this curriculum valued autonomy. The system of designing and implementing this curriculum was a shared responsibility across educational hierarchies. The teachers I was talking to shared that they had fallen into a slump teaching their subjects in the same manner for several decades but felt reborn when they got to take charge of this curriculum. A Happiness Curriculum Committee was formed, which comprised policy makers, SEL experts, and other government officials, but it also had regular schoolteachers, who were directly responsible for the implementation of this programme. From what I understood, these ‘mentor teachers’ (yes, they got a new name!) were assigned a bunch of schools each and were responsible to ensure the teachers in each of those schools get equipped to deliver this new curriculum. The teachers could contact the mentor teachers directly and they, in turn, had to report directly to the Committee. In addition, they shared, almost with pride, that neither the teacher, nor the mentor teacher had to go through the principal anymore. I remember what one of them said, “It’s like a bypass. It’s more efficient!”

It was magically inspiring for me to see how autonomy can turn around the self-determination of an individual and push them towards a growth mindset with such ease.

Neeraja Raghavan, founder-director of Thinking Teacher and a regular writer for Teacher Plus, recently brought out an excellent book titled A Learning Community of Reflective Teachers. It documents what happens when a group of teachers across the country decide to start an initiative to collaborate and learn together. Through discussions, debates, and action research projects that these teachers lead, the book showcases a solid argument towards a new way forward for teacher development where teachers drive it themselves, with the support of each other, always focussing on the voice of the teacher.

I co-design the Artful Teacher Workshop Programme, which has a strong focus on empowering teachers to build agency and be proactive in reclaiming our autonomy. How? By engaging with ourselves through the arts. By pushing ourselves out of the comfort zones we’ve slipped into. By building a tribe of teachers who meet regularly to learn together. By agreeing to open our classrooms to each other, and be ready to build a space of support, trust, and vulnerability. To make conscious, determined efforts, within the four walls of our classroom, to experiment, play, build confidence, and articulation, allowing ourselves to experience first-hand the joy and freedom that taking ownership offers.

So, when did we become non-thinkers?

Today, we perceive teachers as executers, instead of thinkers. Remember the village masterji? He (unfortunately, always male!) was always a friend, philosopher, and guide to all the villagers, with an expansive vision and an imagination to think beyond the obvious. He set a living example of having a positive relationship with learning, and developing one’s innate intelligence to be able to offer progressive solutions to complex problems that a village would face with changing times.

With the advancement of the colonial system of education, the life of the local community found no reflection in the school’s curriculum. In their view, indigenous knowledge and culture were ‘deficient’ and the school curriculum became totally disassociated from the Indian child’s everyday life. Moreover, the teacher had no say in the selection of knowledge and his low salary and status ensured that he would not exercise any professional autonomy or even have a professional identity. (Krishna Kumar, 1991)

At the brink of independence, when there was a nation-wide Community Development Programme (CDP), the village teacher was fondly remembered again! He (still remained male…we’re talking 1952) played an essential role in the attempt to make their community self-sufficient and self-reliant. This programme gave complete autonomy to the villagers and only acted to ‘assist’ the village in this process. (Publications Division, 1959) I’m not sure how successful this programme was over the decades that followed, but we can take a moment to focus on the fact that at a time when we were finding ways to build a nation, our thinkers having experienced what it meant to finally be free, believed, not in a directed top-down approach, but in a way forward that had autonomy at its core.

Today, as the world is going to war with each other and world leaders (not thinkers) are celebrating blood baths, the ongoing colonial system of education works in their favour, training generation after generation to be obedient and submissive. Perhaps, it’s time for a quiet, gentle revolution to spark off where teachers – the real thinkers – see the four walls of their classroom not as a limitation but as a safe space to ignite thought and thinking within our learners. I’ll end with Paulo Freire, with his skillful ways of showing us which direction to follow: “Reading the world always precedes reading the word, and reading the word implies continually reading the world.” It’s time for education to prioritize this.

References

  • Krishna Kumar, 1991, 2005, Political Agenda of Education, Sage Publications
  • Publications Division, 1959, Village Teacher’s Role in Community Development, Govt of India Press
  • Neeraja Raghavan, 2024, A Learning Community of Reflective Teachers, Routledge India
  • Paulo Freire, 1970, Pedagogy of the Oppressed.

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator, and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Can a curriculum make teachers feel reborn? Timira explores how autonomy, not obedience, can transform classrooms, and the people in them.

item.title: Thinking about thinking
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

How does learning happen? We may say it happens through discovery, exploration, experimentation, through exposure to information or experience. There are several ways. However, at the core of it, only when we think and reflect about it does learning happen. Learning is a consequence of thinking. This brings me to the fascinating topic of thinking about thinking, which we almost never think about!

Where does thinking happen? Why is thinking important? Do we think all the time? Do we all think in the same way? Are there different ways of thinking? In what language do we think? Can we think without a language? Do creatures other than humans think? If they do, how do they think? Can we improve the way we think?

I’m going to borrow parts of a brilliant book, Philosophy for Children, written by philosopher and writer, Professor Sundar Sarukkai, which has a chapter on ‘thinking’ that beautifully breaks down this process for us.

Let’s begin with the most basic question that Sarukkai asks – what do you do when you think? If you have to explain what you do when you are eating, it’s easy. You take some food, put it in your mouth, then bite and chew, and finally swallow it. Similarly, you can explain step-by-step what you do when you run, jump, cycle, etc.

Take a moment and try to describe what it is that you do when you think!

It’s difficult, isn’t it! Sarukkai encourages the reader to note down what happens to your body when you are thinking. He asks if there is a place in your body where you do your thinking. We may have been taught that we think in our mind or brain, but can you feel your brain thinking just like you can feel that your mouth and throat are cold when you are eating ice-cream? Is it possible that we can also think in our heart? Or in our stomach – after all it does rumble when we are hungry and act funny just before the math exam! Sarukkai provokes the reader further by asking several questions like – when you run, your body sweats; when you think, does your body sweat? Do you feel tired? What part of your body becomes active when you are thinking? We can say that thinking is talking or arguing with yourself. But for a cricketer, it’s the body that thinks when a ball is coming their way – they have no time to talk to themselves in that moment!

There are endless ways humans think and the better we know how we think, the better we learn. This curiosity to understand HOW we think has led several educators and researchers to design tools to make thinking visible. Project Zero by Harvard University is one such initiative where they designed several Thinking Routines to create cultures of thinking within schools.

A social studies teacher in class 2 teaching a unit on ‘types of houses’ recently shared this image in her class (please add the AI image) and used a popular thinking routine called See-Think-Wonder. She first asked the children to state ‘What it is they SEE’ in the image. Students threw up answers like:

‘a city with skyscrapers and jhopdis’

‘an old city and a new city’

‘different kinds of houses and buildings’

‘brick houses with roofs and tall glass buildings’

‘colourful houses and blue and grey buildings’

This was a great entry point into the topic. As the image reflected a stark difference in the types of houses, she then asked the children ‘Why do you THINK there are such different kinds of houses in a city?’ The responses of students as young as these were absolutely intriguing.

‘…because rich people live in tall buildings and poor people live in the small ones.’

Teacher: “What makes you say that?”

‘…because tall buildings are fancy and expensive. It doesn’t take a lot of money to make small houses like in the picture.’

Another response was:

‘…we need different types of houses now because tall buildings have more flats.’

Teacher: “…and why do we need more flats? What makes you say that?”

‘…because there are so many more people living in the city than before.’

Another child added in:

‘…yes, my mother always says that when she was my age there was not so much traffic, so many cars, so many people in Mumbai.’

The teacher layered the conversation with an additional thinking routine ‘What makes you say that?’ allowing the learners to make the thoughts behind their thinking visible. The discussion ranged from how the city was changing to socio-economic realities, also making room for personal experiences to come in. Many students talked about re-development of their own building – a dinner-table conversation in every home in a city like Mumbai.

The teacher decided to end the class by giving the third part of the thinking routine as homework. She asked the learners to write a list of questions starting with: “I WONDER who/why/what/which/where/how…” using any of these question starters. This allowed absolute freedom of one’s curiosity to know beyond what is obvious and students were so excited to do this homework, many of them began right away even before the class was over.

Of course, the teacher would find her way to stilt houses, igloos, and mud huts and all the other things mentioned in the syllabus, but to use this opportunity to get learners thinking ensured they were invested on this learning journey. There are several such thinking routines (think-pair-share, think-puzzle-explore, circle of viewpoints–a resource link is provided at the end of the article), each with their own objectives and provocations but all with the single aim to encourage students to be curious and for them to see and know what and how they are thinking about things.

Unfortunately, we don’t draw much attention to the quality of questions we ask in the class. The questions given at the end of a textbook chapter are usually recall-based, closed questions and a few reasoning questions, which the learner is expected to answer with reference to the given text. None of these encourage you to think. And to top it all is our new friend AI, which almost encourages you not to think!

Over the last few years, I have been encouraging teachers to identify the ‘nature’ of the questions before crafting them in order to make conscious efforts to bring about deeper thinking. It requires a little bit of extra work, in an already very busy life of a teacher, to create a set of ‘thinking questions’ for every lesson plan. Take a look at the shift in the nature of questions in a grade 5 and a nursery class and you’ll get an idea of the impact it can have on thinking. The quality of learning in the class improved dramatically as teachers became conscious of the kinds of questions students were asking. After the initial rigour of consciously wading through this new competency that was thrown at them, it soon became an effortless way of being. They couldn’t ask a closed, recall-based question without noticing it anymore!

Bringing about cultures of thinking in schools is the need of the hour and it is essential that teachers themselves internalize this culture first. As part of a programme I co-design, we introduced Socratic Seminars with teachers so they can experience how exciting and enriching a collaborative thinking process can be. A Socratic Seminar is a structured discussion method, where participants collaboratively explore a piece of text through thoughtful questioning and dialogue. It's not a debate, but rather a process of collective meaning-making where participants analyze, interpret, and build upon each other's ideas.

A teacher shared this after their first Socratic Seminar experience: “For the very first time ever I participated and engaged in a Socratic Seminar. It gave me an opportunity to understand and learn how to analyze complex texts and questions…. fostering my own critical thinking and to engage in meaningful dialogue…..which in turn prompted me on my own questioning techniques.” Another teacher said: “When we get into discussions, we are always arguing, agreeing, and disagreeing on viewpoints. This was the first time I felt all of us were interested in figuring out together what the article was trying to say. The format of the seminar pushes you to be a better listener and build on other’s ideas instead of countering them. Made me think of discussions in a new light!”

Learning as a collective endeavour in an educational setting is ideal. It allows for participants, both adults and children, to actively engage in discussion, critique, and refinement of ideas and thought. If learning is a consequence of thinking, then perhaps we can begin to see thinking too as a collective enterprise. A tiny big shift in our classrooms – ready to give it a shot! What do you think?

References

  • Philosophy for Children, Sundar Sarukkai, published by Ektara
  • Project Zero – Harvard University, https://pz.harvard.edu/thinking-routines

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator, and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD in teacher education. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Can we feel our brain the way we feel ice-cream? Timira invites you to think about thinking itself.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: thanks for writing and sharing a good article.

item.title: On experiencing learning experiences!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

I recently ate at a special Gujarati thali restaurant that has been around since before independence. It is special, not only because of the absolutely indulgent food they serve but the manner in which they serve you. It feels like home, with a team of people serving you the hottest puri that comes out of the kitchen, explaining to you the seasonal ingredients that are part of each dish, and they don’t let you leave till you have tasted every single dessert they have to offer! What an experience that was and it made me think about what it is in an experience that makes us remember it. Why? Because as an educator, I see ‘teaching’ as an act of creating ‘learning experiences’ for students. Experiences they will enjoy, cherish and REMEMBER, not to vomit in a test, but connect to other experiences that life throws at them.

There are a couple of threads here that I’m going to chase. One is that of experience itself and what it entails. Second, how the arts enable a perception within us that influences our context through which we experience life. And finally, in the context of education, to view learning as an act of engaging with life through dynamic experiences and how this view can bring about a pedagogical shift.

An experience

John Dewey has written at length about the nature of experience in his book Art As Experience (1934), where he says that an ordinary experience, like living life, occurs continuously through the interaction between a live creature (an individual) and its environment. However, ‘an experience’ occurs and stands out in an ongoing experience because it is marked from what happened before and what came after; it has a beginning, middle, and an end. For example, ‘that meal’, ‘that conversation’, ‘that storm’ and so on.

Dewey adds another interesting layer here that the background or context of the world the individual inhabits shapes their experience, which is impregnated with one’s uniqueness. For instance, an individual’s interests, dispositions, physical, cognitive, and emotional capacities and so on (Dewey, 1934) will influence how they experience something. Thus, different people will explore an experience through their uniqueness, giving rise to different perceptions.

From a Deweyan point of view, this unique experience that he calls an ‘aesthetic experience’ roughly follows a structure. The experience is set off by some material or a stimulus like opening a book, viewing an image, beginning a meal, or starting a conversation. The effect of this stimulus changes depending on the context of the individual engaging with it, and it merges into new ones as the experience proceeds. When these different parts form a distinctive unity that stands out from the general experiential stream of our lives, Dewey says the experience in question is aesthetic.

For a moment, let’s see how this can pan out in the context of learning. For a child, the general experiential stream of their life is going to school every day. But the moment something different occurs – like a quarrel during lunch, a teacher wearing something special, a math class conducted on the playground, a language lesson that begins with a puzzle or a game – a certain stimulus is set off with the possibility of creating a unique and aesthetic experience.

Aesthetic and non-aesthetic experiences

Dewey uses the example of a journey to differentiate between an aesthetic experience and a non-aesthetic one. In an aesthetic experience, means and ends coalesce. Means are an end in the aesthetic. While in non-aesthetic experiences, means are mechanical steps used to get to the desired end. There is delight in moving about and seeing in an aesthetic journey, while in a non-aesthetic one the only objective is to reach the destination and any way to shorten the trip is welcome.

Young students learning mathematical operations for the first time will gladly spend hours repeatedly adding the number 3, 20 times (3 + 3 = 6 + 3 = 9 + 3 = 12…), making mistakes, starting over, helping each other, feeling the joy of getting it right, and then slowly feeling the tedium of the exercise when they must do the same for number 4 and 5 and so on. As it is a new experience for them, they will jump right into it, but there is a moment in this journey where it can become mechanical with energies dispersing and distractions coming in. Before such a moment occurs, the teacher must introduce the concept of multiplication, triggering off a new experiential cycle of discovery.

Well-designed learning experiences where the teacher is acutely conscious of how and when she plays with the material or stimulus that sets off experiences for students to be engaged and invested in, while being mindful of the context in which they will be experiencing it, is a palpable shift in pedagogy.

The arts enabling aesthetic experiences

We are hoping to create educational models that follow a curriculum framework where the focus is on moving about, seeing, exploring, being curious, and engaging with the world for learning to happen; where arts-based approaches full of material and external stimulus play an important role in the learning process. However, educator and philosopher Gert Biesta is critical of the growing attention the arts has in educational circles these days. He highlights it as a serious concern that schools want art on their curriculum because it is useful: “Playing music will make you better at algebra, dance will make you better at geometry, theatre will improve your self-regulation skills,” and so on (Biesta, & Skregelid, 2022, Art keeps us in and with the World).

Biesta points out that when we talk about the usefulness of the arts for generating particular educational “outcomes,” there is a disappearance of the arts from arts education as the focus is on what arts education ‘makes or produces’ instead of it focussing on ‘what it makes possible’. This directly co-relates to the non-aesthetic experience we discussed earlier, which focuses on the end destination, as described by Dewey.

So how can we talk about the importance of the arts without talking about its usefulness? Here, Biesta draws inspiration from artists who are, in different ways, always trying to figure out what it means to exist ‘in and with the world’ and what it means to be human and to live one’s life with its many dimensions, manifestations, and facets, knowing well that it is impossible to arrive at a definitive answer. What distinguishes art is that art always has a medium: sound, paint, stone, time, story, images, words, tone, rhythm, movement, and so on. So, in working with a “medium,” artists are stepping into a dialogue with the world outside of them.

‘I tend to think that the arts are relevant for education – which is different from saying that they are useful for education – because the arts themselves have the existential question, the question of what it means to exist in and with the world, as their central concern. And for me, this is also the central concern for education’ (Biesta, & Skregelid, 2022, Art keeps us in and with the World).

When a teacher brings in a stimulus or some material (a photograph, a news article, a book, an object) to start off a conversation in the class, this can be seen as the “medium” that gives the opportunity to students to engage with the world and the existential questions that come with it.

Experience. The arts. Where’s the teacher in all of this?

Biesta says that educational work is about ‘holding’ students in this difficult middle ground where they can meet the world and can meet themselves in relation to the world. Where they can encounter the difference between ‘fantasy’ and ‘reality’, ‘desire’ and ‘consequences of such desires’. This way of looking at education suggests that education is not about designing smooth, personalized learning trajectories that rush students swiftly towards the production of measurable learning outcomes. It rather suggests that if at its core, education takes on the existential question seriously, it seeks to interrupt as it “stages” an encounter between students and reality. This interruption must be followed by suspension, by slowing down, so there is time to meet this reality and work through the complexities and possible frustrations of such a meeting. At this stage, teachers need to provide sustenance through tools and support for holding students in that place. The arts offer this journey and for educators, the main task is to open up different “existential possibilities” for students – different ways in which they can exist, meet the world, and meet themselves in relation to the world.

However, in order for teachers to truly bring these nuanced ideas into their pedagogical approach, it is imperative for them to be inundated with learning experiences of this nature. Teacher development programs need to use the medium of the arts to hold teachers in that ‘difficult middle ground’ where they can meet the world and meet themselves in relation to the world. This will equip them to ask difficult questions, not fear interruptions but see them as opportunities to engage, to slow down and bring attention to such encounters, and finally learn what sustenance can look like preparing themselves to design similar learning experiences for students.

Tying it back to Dewey who said that individual contexts influence how we receive experiences around us, we can perhaps hope that engaging with the medium of the arts regularly can enable an aesthetic perception that leaves teachers open and ready to engage with the world, thus influencing their individual context, where the delight in seeing and exploring through the journey is more important than reaching a destination.

References

  • Dewey, J. (1934), Art As Experience.
  • Biesta, G. J. J. & Skregelid, L. (2022). Art keeps us in and with the World: Gert Biesta in Conversation with LisbetSkregelid.

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD in teacher education. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Education is more than measurable outcomes. Timira shares how classrooms can create experiences that students engage with and truly remember.

item.title: When classrooms turn students into explorers!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

I loved vacations as a child. Vacations allowed us to do something different from the usual school life that always came with a strict timetable and a ticking clock. Vacations were like very long extended weekends where we could do what we wanted. But what was most exciting during vacations was if there was a ‘trip’. It was a thing – children had to be engaged differently during vacations, and it was best if it involved a ‘trip to somewhere’.

Sometimes it was as ordinary as visiting a part of the city that was far from where we lived. For many, visiting their hometown was the norm. For some, it meant going for a holiday to another city or even another country! But what was common between all these endeavours was the idea of travel.

As we return from the holiday season, I think about how ‘travel’ can become an opportunity to engage with the world and how we can bring attention to deepening this experience of meaningful engagement. Travel may sound like a luxury of the privileged, but why should we allow the capitalist view to dictate how we define and ‘consume’ travel. A train ride to one’s hometown or even just to the other end of the city, or a ride on the new metro can be seen as travel. In an age, where travel has become primarily about taking selfies for social media, where the name of the destination defines whether it is even worthy of sharing, it is perhaps worth assessing how our own relationship with travel has shifted. With staring into screens winning over gazing out of windows, and virtual chat rooms replacing conversations with travel companions, could this be an opportunity for education to step in and offer a new lens to look through?

This is why I love educators. We identify a problem, a disequilibrium of sorts and then take it very seriously because we know that here's an opportunity to bring in a new lens, a new perspective, a new pathway leading to several rabbit holes to jump into. And what can be most exciting is that we do this with a bunch of young minds who perhaps have larger capacities to see the world in new ways! This is when education becomes a process of engaging with the world, where learning is a given, not a measurable outcome.

If we are talking about travel as a means to engage with the world, it is imperative that I write about the brilliant Canadian author, illustrator, and conceptual artist, Keri Smith and her wonderful book How to be an Explorer of the World. The title says it all, doesn’t it! This is a book of 59 ideas of how to engage with your surroundings in novel ways. Take a look at the list below, which Smith scribbled in the middle of a sleepless night, which lead to the idea of this book.

Imagine stepping out of your house to buy onions, with just a few of these 13 lenses this list provides. The little walk down your street and back will transform your experience of it. Buying onions will no longer be the chore it possibly is right now! Can you imagine what will occur when we provide such tools to young minds (while also getting them to go buy the onions next time!) We may lose a few children to the world of curiosities (and no guarantee that you’ll get your onions!) When such tools become a lens, a way of seeing, the world opens up in expansive ways that allows all individuals, not only children, to stay connected to their surroundings, to life and to their sense of presence – which we are losing rapidly trying to catch up with the ‘smart world’. The author puts it delightfully in her preface when she says, “the real learning…is to be had in the midst of living!”

Travel can be skillfully extrapolated to the idea of new sights, tastes, rituals, languages, and cultural nuances through photography, travel magazines, travel and food shows, documentary films, and even audio podcasts that hold possibilities of exposure that a classroom setup cannot otherwise offer. I’ve seen a geography teacher artfully use an episode of a travel and food show exploring Kerala to introduce a unit on coastal plains. The conversations and curiosities with which the students entered this unit allowed a richness to the topic that simply teaching out of a textbook or PowerPoint presentation would never enable. Many years ago, I was introduced to a television series titled Raja, Rasoi Aur Anya Kahaniya by a history teacher who used it while exploring the Mauryan Empire. Her students were asked to watch a particular episode as homework and pick out information they got from the episode to add to their understanding of that period and its influences in present day India. When watching TV becomes homework, we have successfully made a shift in the narrative of what learning can look like!

I’d like to go back to the idea of travel, of actually going from one place to another – not necessarily from a familiar to an unfamiliar place, but simply journeying with intentionality. Private schools organize educational trips for their students with similar objectives, to create shared learning experiences, but these are not always affordable by all and has the potential to exclude. The fact remains that everyone travels, to go somewhere, near or far, new or old, and education can make this experience more meaningful in various ways. I remember reading the poem A Railway Carriage by Robert Louis Stevenson with class 4 students, when I set them off to write their own little poems of what they remember seeing outside a train window while travelling to some place. Most children who visited their hometown during the vacation had much to write about, while the city-bound children wrote vividly about observations within the city that daily train commuters would never even have noticed!

Equipping our little explorers with simple tools like the ones Smith suggests in her book, we educators can cleverly weave this idea of ‘seeing’ into the curriculum. Instead of giving math sums and essay writing as ‘holiday homework’, can we get children to walk around with journals, pens, and empty pockets collecting, drawing, searching, surveying, observing, asking, questioning, and in turn encouraging them to engage with their world in novel ways?

As educators, a big responsibility is how we honour what they bring back to the class in ways that are embedded in curiosity and respect. How can we ensure that the stories being shared aren’t a display of what their social status can access but about how they engaged with the world they set out to explore? I can envision large displays across a classroom wall that maps the various experiences of a group of children who perhaps explored languages and names of things and people on one such vacation or another exploration of patterns that everyone decided to explore.

In a class of 30-40 students, where each inhabits a world that is starkly different from the other, it is indeed unfortunate that we often forget the diversity that a classroom offers us. The nature of such explorations allows for meaning making that is embedded in one’s own context, own experience and in a lived reality that is local. With the obsession to make education “global”, knowledge building and learning are becoming homogenous in our classrooms, just like our cities, our food, our language and our capacities to see. It is essential that we do not forget the significance of indigenous systems of knowledge, local ecologies and intelligences, our truths and our contexts, and its absolute importance in learning and understanding the world at large. And the first step towards that could be to travel through it all like an explorer with a keen eye, a pen and journal, and large empty pockets!

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD in teacher education. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Beyond the old debate about travel vs tourism, Timira shares how educators can transform holiday homework into immersive and exploratory activities.

item.title: What it means to be “in your element”
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

Mamta, a 25-year-old sprightly, single mother to a 6-year-old, walks into my house every morning at 10:30 with her beaming smile and infectious enthusiasm asking, “Didi, aaj kya banau?” This, after having finished cooking in three households before mine. Mamta cooks about 5-6 dishes, from prepping to washing up and leaving the kitchen squeaky clean – all in less than an hour. I often watch her in the kitchen. It is like watching a beautifully choreographed piece of performance. Everything is laid out, ingredients, tools, vessels. The two gas burners are put to work immediately while her hands are busy mixing, kneading, chopping in a way that not a minute is wasted. While something is marinating, there’s a dish cooking. While something is cooking, she’s chopping for the next dish. And while things are simmering, she’s washing up and answering her daughter’s phone call to let her know that she’ll be home soon. Her movements around the kitchen carry the ease and grace of an artist. When Mamta is in the kitchen, she is truly “in her element”!

So, what does it mean to be in your element? When was the last time you felt you were “in your element”? What were you doing at the time? It could be that history class in which you had every single child engaged and hooked and you just knew how you had cracked that class! Or it could be the time you single-handedly organized a huge family celebration and felt completely chuffed at its success.

Ken Robinson does an excellent job breaking down this idea in his book titled The Element – How Finding Your Passion Changes Everything. He says:

“When people are in their Element, they connect with something fundamental to their sense of identity, purpose, and well-being. Being there provides a sense of self-revelation, of defining who they really are and what they’re really meant to be doing with their lives. The Element is the meeting point between natural aptitude and personal passion.”

I took the liberty to draw it out in the Venn diagram below:

When personal and professional unite

Most teachers sit in this sweet intersection above. But it could also be a spot in which we can find ourselves stuck. Teachers have a professional identity attached to being ‘passionate’ about the work we do. Sometimes that passion gets chipped away, but we continue doing the work under the pretense of loving what we do. The reason why I am writing about this in my column that focuses on the ‘Teacher Within’ is for us to recognize that we are dynamic individuals with expansive interests, capacities and potentials, and the attempt here is to find different ways to keep engaging with ourselves and our varied passions, get closer to our authentic selves and slowly and skillfully erase the lines drawn between who we are as individuals and who we identify as professionals in the classroom. How can our individual selves feed our professional selves and how can we take what we learn as professionals into our personal lives?

Teachers have often been told to leave their personal lives at the school gate. I tend to disagree because it makes teachers inauthentic and pushes them away from being ‘in their element’. It is deeply important for young people to experience authenticity when they engage with adults in their lives.

I get it. I love it. I WANT it. But HOW?

We all have a natural aptitude towards certain things and usually these aptitudes are recognized when we are young. I remember my brother was someone who loved pulling things apart and putting them back together – the radio, the Walkman we shared, my talking doll, parts of his cycle. While I was always creating things that didn’t exist – new board games, new ways of eating the same old food, handmade notebooks with all kinds of different pages, I could go on. Children explore their natural aptitude quite organically; the more they explore, the more they develop their skills; and as they get better at it, they begin to enjoy it and love it. However, as we grow into adulthood, we begin to lose touch with our natural aptitudes and tend to work towards developing skills that seem to be ‘beneficial’ to us. It’s probably why the world is full of adults who are perhaps good at what they do but they don’t really love doing it. What is wonderful about our aptitudes is that we may have several over our lifetime and exploring them allows us to engage with our growing and changing selves in intimate ways.

Robinson, in his book, adds two interesting aspects that could aid in finding our Element – attitude and opportunity. I’d like to bring Mamta back here. At 25, she already knows that even though she loves cooking and is proud of what she puts out every day, she’s not going to love this all her life. She wants to train to be a make-up artist. At this point, she doesn’t know if she has an aptitude for it, but her passion is enough for her to pursue it. She’s been attending night school all of last year and is giving her class 10 board exams as I write this article. Why? Because the course she wants to join requires her to complete class 12, so she’s determined to give it another two years and complete her education in order to pursue this. In addition, it’s an expensive programme, so she’s been taking puranpoli and modak orders to make the extra income she needs for the course. This is the attitude that Robinson talks about.

“Attitude is our personal perspective on our selves and our circumstances – our angle on things, our disposition, and emotional point of view. Many things affect our attitudes, including our basic character, our spirit, our sense of self-worth, the perceptions of those around us, and their expectations of us.”

“How we perceive our circumstances and how we create and take opportunities depends largely on what we expect of ourselves.”

I draw immense strength and inspiration from women like Mamta who help us examine what our relationship with ourselves and our expectations of ourselves is. How much our view of our self is influenced or determined by others’ expectations and perceptions of us? Remember the year you got a subject you didn’t know much about and felt very incompetent to teach it? Or a school event which you were pushed into that was totally out of your forte? You were caught in that place where you love what you do, but suddenly, you weren’t very good at it. Try to recall what you did to overcome that?

I remember asking a pre-school teacher to teach science to grade 3 for one semester. She had to teach the topic on birds and confessed she didn’t know much beyond the local crow, pigeon, and house sparrow. Well, she could have just opened the textbook and taught what was in there, but Ms. K was a teacher who liked being in her element. Weekend mornings were spent going for bird walks; she joined every forum there was about local Mumbai birds, brought out her old dusty binoculars, picked out all the bird books from the school library and within weeks, she realized she was a deeply passionate bird enthusiast and had never quite known this about herself!

Another teacher, a complete non-reader, joined the reading programme in school and discovered a world of solitude in books. She said she always found reading boring as a child, but suddenly here she was realizing that books created a world for her that helped her switch off from a hectic school day and relieve her from her demanding joint family. Today, she’s part of a reading club in Mumbai that spends a few hours every week reading amidst Nature, within the city, together, but in silence.

I’ll never forget the teacher suffering from severe insomnia, who decided to fight her doctors and family by refusing medication and instead trusted the arts to help her body reset her sleeping pattern. As part of the Artful teacher workshop programme, she picked up Mandala drawing as her Personal Creative Practice and spent her waking hours of the night drawing mandalas which relaxed her nerves, brought down her anxiety around sleep, and after several months of practice, it eventually helped her get her sleep back.

Get to know the teacher within

Being in your element depends a lot on your relationship with yourself. Take time to build a deep relationship with yourself. Spend time alone. Do things on your own. Learn new things. Explore new interests. Step out of your comfort zone. Don’t judge yourself or your age! My friend’s 65-year-old Malayali mother decided she wanted to learn German one day and travelled across the city twice a week to learn alongside a class full of 20-year-olds. She didn’t find her element in the German language; she perhaps found her element in the act of showing up for herself!

New opportunities can help us teachers understand that our identity is not limited to what we teach and how we teach it. It is connected to who we are as individuals, how we engage with the world, what moves us, and what our deepest passions are. This will feed into who we are and expand our own perceptions of our self. When these aspects of life come alive, they will find their way into the classroom in ways that may surprise us at first, but soon become an unconscious part of an authentic self we learn to carry with us wherever we go.

Are you listening to you?

We are all beings who are constantly growing, and it is imperative to continuously engage with our self to get to know what the current version is like. Let’s not take our likes and dislikes, comforts and discomforts for granted. If you were terrible at drawing as a child, it doesn’t mean you still are. The more we engage with our self, the more we begin to trust our own intuition. A sense we know so little about. Yet, as teachers we use it so much to make micro decisions all the time. Tagore strongly believed that a teacher must be guided by their gut. I’d like to end with a piece of his advice to one of his teachers, “Do not be preoccupied with method. Leave your instincts to guide you to life. Children differ from one another. One must learn to know them, to navigate among them as one navigates among reefs. To explore the geography of their minds, a mysterious instinct, sympathetic to life, is the best of all guides.”

When we doubt ourselves, we feel unsure about decisions that come out of our intuition. And we doubt our intuition when we stop engaging with ourselves in intimate truthful ways. Being one with our instinct is a wonderful way to be in our element.

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD in teacher education. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
When do we truly feel like ourselves? As adults, we often drift away from our natural aptitudes in our pursuit of practical and stable careers. What if the key to fulfillment lies not in doing more, but in rediscovering what already lights us up? But reconnecting with our forgotten interests brings us back “in our element”, an ongoing relationship with oneself, not a fixed destination.
